title: Cat Facial Swelling and Hives: Urgent Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Sudden facial swelling or hives in cats can be life-threatening. Learn why this is an emergency and how to triage your pet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden facial swelling in cats is a high-priority emergency typically caused by an acute allergic reaction that can obstruct the airway. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat has swelling, hives, or difficulty breathing.

Why is my cat's face suddenly swelling and breaking out in hives?

Sudden swelling of the face, muzzle, or eyes, often accompanied by raised bumps called hives (urticaria), typically indicates an acute allergic reaction. This is an overreaction of your cat's immune system to a trigger, such as an insect sting, a new medication, a vaccination, or an environmental allergen.

Is sudden facial swelling in my cat a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Yes, sudden facial swelling is considered a veterinary emergency.
  • While the swelling itself is visible on the outside, it can also occur internally in the throat or airway.
  • Internal swelling can lead to life-threatening difficulty breathing or anaphylactic shock.
  • If your cat is lethargic, vomiting, or struggling to breathe, seek immediate emergency care.

Why should I take a photo of my cat's allergic reaction?

  • Taking a clear photo of your cat's face and any visible hives can be incredibly helpful for your veterinarian.
  • Allergic reactions can change rapidly; a photo provides a baseline to determine if the swelling is progressing or receding.
  • If you are using a telehealth service, this image allows the professional to assess the severity of the reaction before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, facial swelling (facial-conjunctival angioedema) and hives (urticaria) are typically manifestations of a Type I hypersensitivity reaction, although not all urticarial reactions are mediated by this mechanism. These reactions can result from vaccine or drug administration, food ingestion (common allergens include fish, beef, milk, and chicken), or insect bites/stings. Facial-conjunctival edema is a more severe presentation than hives alone and may be associated with mild to moderately severe systemic anaphylaxis. In severe cases of urticaria or anaphylaxis, immediate treatment with epinephrine (SC at 1:1,000 dilution, 0.1-0.5 mL) is indicated, potentially repeated every 10-20 minutes, or if given IV, diluted to 1:10,000 with vigilant cardiovascular monitoring; IV fluids are also indicated to prevent vascular collapse. Antihistamines and corticosteroids can also be considered.

Chapter: Immunology, Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 824)

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden hives in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common triggers include stings from bees or wasps, reactions to recent vaccinations, or contact with certain plants and chemicals.

faqs.question: Should I wait to see if the swelling goes down?
faqs.answer: No. Because allergic reactions can progress to airway obstruction or shock within minutes, you should contact a veterinarian immediately.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat human antihistamines?
faqs.answer: Do not administer any medication without professional guidance, as dosages for cats differ significantly from humans and some products contain toxic additives.
